OGC
Feature
T
he energy production industry in Southeast Texas is a mul-
tibillion dollar business, and as such can fall prey to crime
and vandalism.
Crime is a huge problem in the oil patch, according to John
Chamberlain, Executive Director of the Energy Security Council, a
Houston-based nonprot organization that aims to educate, train and
form alliances with security professionals in the energy sector. But right
now, he said, there is no centralized database for reporting criminal
activity and sabotage. Rather, anyone who works in energy produc-
tion — from the Eagle Ford Shale, which occupies land across some
30 dierent counties, to small contracting units — reports crime and
vandalism to their local sheri’s precinct. Depending on the dollar
value of the the, in many cases the crimes do not get reported at all.
e Railroad Commission of Texas, the regulatory agency that oversees
energy production in the state, does not compile these statistics.
Glenn Miers, executive vice president of sales and marketing
for Innovative Surveillance Solutions, Inc., a security company based
in Tyler, Texas, makes it his business to get at the heart of the crime
problem in the patch.
“In the state of Texas alone, in June 2006, crime reached propor-
tions of at least $70 million per month. It’s an ongoing risk whether
you’re drilling, excavating or producing. Even construction companies
working for contractors have to keep their eye on everything from
vehicles to tools, equipment and laptop computers.”
According to Miers, “crime is a major risk that goes un-talked
about for the most part.” Money is the driving force behind most
crimes in this industry. e fact that facilities are located in remote
areas where the closest sheri’s department could be 45 minutes away
adds to the burden.
“A l o t o f c om p a n i e s d o n ’ t r e p o r t s m a l l e r c r i m e s ,” s a i d C h a m -
berlain. “To them, the is just the cost of doing business in the oen
remote oil patch.” It does not have to be that way, however, he said.
When it comes to the and sabotage on energy production elds,
there is certainly a criminal element at play. ese sites oen have a lot
of equipment lying around, said Carl Bowen, Sergeant of Investigations
at the DeWitt County Sheri’s oce.
“A piece of equipment can go missing for weeks before anyone
even notices it’s gone.” But tools, equipment and vehicles are not the
only items that can have legs.
“One of the biggest things I see,” said Bowen, “is the of informa-
tion. Even a decade ago foremen were walking around their sites with a
clipboard in hand. Now everyone has a laptop carrying very sensitive,
very valuable information. Not only do you have data about your site,
but you’ve got information on your employees, tool serial numbers,
permits, and more.”
“While in Southeast Texas we are seeing some illegal trade
between the States and Mexico,” said Bowen, site security managers
should also have rival company employees on their radar. “We just
indicted a subcontractor that went to a rival, hooked his vehicle up to
equipment and got caught towing it away.”
Hiring a security company to implement anti-the protocols is
probably the least time consuming thing to prevent on-site crime. Miers
explains that a company like Innovative Surveillance will hire security
personnel and conduct background checks. ey will also implement
procedures for keeping track of everyone coming and going from the site.
Another great deterrent to oileld crime is use of the Energy
Crime Stoppers Program, which is funded by the Energy Security
Council. Chamberlain acknowledges that not all contractors in the
eld are a large enough operation to warrant employing a security
company, however every company can aord to post Energy Crime
Stopper signs, he said.
One of the simplest tools out there for crime prevention, adds
Chamberlain, is prominently posting Crime Stopper signs. e Energy
Crime Stoppers toll free hotline encourages individuals to call in
anonymous tips.
“Tipsters receive cash rewards for information leading to
arrest and/or conviction for theft, fraud or crimes committed
against energy companies,” according to a press release about
the program. The hotline nets around 12 to 20 calls per month.
For more information on the Energy Crime Stoppers hotline,
visit http://www.energysecuritycouncil.org/
